data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-16T01:13:33.776Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating .NET Framework code that uses Thread.Abort to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it uses `Thread`, `Thread.Start()`, `Thread.Join()`, and `Thread.Sleep()`, but does NOT actually use `Thread.Abort`, `ThreadAbortException`, `Thread.ResetAbort`, or `Thread.Interrupt` for thread termination.

According to the available skill description for `thread-abort-migration`:
- USE FOR: modernizing code that calls Thread.Abort, catching ThreadAbortException, etc.
- DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration.


Since this code only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep without any abort or interrupt patterns, I don't need to invoke the skill and can just explain directly to the user that their code doesn't actually require migration for these particular APIs.
